content.text: Two methods on the event object let you take control of what happens after a user interaction.
stops the browser's built-in response to an event.
stops the event from traveling up the DOM to parent handlers. They solve different problems and are often confused with each other.

attrs.alt: Event bubbling from button through p and div — and how stopPropagation blocks it at the button level
attrs.title: stopPropagation vs default bubbling

content.text: Event Bubbling: Why the Methods Exist
When a user clicks an element, the browser fires the event on that element, then on its parent, then its grandparent, all the way to the document root. This is called
. If every ancestor has an onClick, they all fire in bottom-up order.

Bubbling is useful — it lets a parent listen to events from all its children without attaching a listener to each one. But sometimes you need to stop the propagation or cancel the browser's default action.
preventDefault: Stop the Browser's Default Response
Some elements have built-in behaviors tied to specific events.
when you want the event to stop after the current element's handler and not reach any ancestor. It does
Browser's default action (navigation, form POST, newline)
Event from bubbling to parent handlers
Both the default action and bubbling

When you need to stop both the default action and the bubbling, call both methods. A common case is a link that opens a modal rather than navigating and should not trigger a parent element's click handler.
If the same element has multiple listeners for the same event (possible when using native DOM
stops bubbling but still lets the remaining same-element listeners fire.
stops both bubbling and any other listeners on the same element.
return false Does Not Work in React
from an event handler does nothing.
// ❌ Does NOT prevent navigation in React { doSomething(); return false; }}> About // ✅ Must call explicitly { event.preventDefault(); doSomething(); }} > About

You can handle all events in a list with one parent handler using event delegation — reading the target to determine which item was interacted with — and stop propagation only when a nested action (like a Delete button) should not trigger the parent.

A very common UI pattern is closing a dropdown when the user clicks anywhere outside it. One approach: listen for clicks on the document and close the dropdown, but stop propagation from the dropdown itself so clicking inside it does not trigger the close.
The click-outside pattern works but has edge cases with portals and third-party code. An alternative is to use a transparent overlay div or the
event on the container, which avoids document-level listeners entirely.
Mistake 1: Using stopPropagation to fix problems caused by poor structure

in many places, it often means the component tree is structured so that events go to handlers they shouldn't. Restructuring — moving the handler closer to the element, or lifting the action up — usually produces cleaner code than scattering stop calls.
Mistake 2: Calling preventDefault on events that don't have a default
Mistake 3: Expecting stopPropagation to affect useEffect listeners
stops the event from reaching other React handlers higher in the tree. However, native listeners added via
at the document level may still fire because React's event delegation and native propagation interact in subtle ways.
to stop the browser from handling the key combination itself (e.g. Ctrl+S tries to save the page, Ctrl+F opens the browser search bar).
To replace the browser's right-click menu with a custom one, prevent the default on the
event and render your own menu at the cursor position.
creates synthetic events. To assert that
were called, spy on the event object.
One of the most common uses of
in React apps is intercepting anchor tag clicks to use a client-side router instead of a full-page navigation. React Router's
does this internally, but knowing the pattern helps when you need it manually.
Notice the guard: modified clicks (Cmd+Click, Ctrl+Click) are not intercepted because the user explicitly wants to open in a new tab or window. Only a plain left click with no modifiers and no

React attaches a single listener at the root element for each event type rather than one per DOM node. This delegation model means stopPropagation and preventDefault interact with React's internal plumbing, not directly with individual DOM nodes.
Understanding delegation matters when mixing React with non-React code or third-party libraries that also manipulate events:
— they fire AFTER React's root handler, so stopPropagation inside React does NOT prevent them from seeing the event.
— a portal renders outside the parent DOM tree but remains inside React's virtual tree, so events bubble through React components naturally even though the DOM parent is elsewhere.
— may call stopPropagation on their own events, making them invisible to React event handlers placed above them in the DOM.
Passive Event Listeners and Scroll Performance

Browsers introduced passive event listeners so they can begin scrolling before calling your handler (since most scroll/touch handlers don't call preventDefault). React uses passive listeners by default for
inside those handlers throws a console warning and has no effect.
If you genuinely need to prevent touch-based scrolling (e.g., a custom swipe carousel), attach a native listener via a ref:
React supports capture-phase versions of every event by appending
, etc. Capture handlers fire top-down (document → target) instead of bottom-up.

inside a capture handler aborts propagation before the event even reaches the target — useful for analytics wrappers that need to observe every click, or modals that must intercept key presses regardless of which child is focused:
cancels the browser's built-in behavior (form navigation, link follow, newline insert) without stopping bubbling.
prevents the event from reaching parent handlers without affecting the browser's default action.
Use both together when you need to cancel the browser default AND prevent parent handlers from firing.
has no effect in React — call the methods explicitly.
is more robust than relying on stopPropagation across React version boundaries.
